config.title: Berlin Ghost Stations: FAQ
config.subtitle: Straight answers on what the ghost stations were, which fifteen stations were sealed, what you can actually visit today, and the one that is closed again.

items.q: What were the Berlin ghost stations?
items.a: They were fifteen U-Bahn and S-Bahn stations that were sealed when the Berlin Wall went up on 13 August 1961. Three routes ran mostly through West Berlin but passed underneath the middle of the city, which was East Berlin territory: the U6, the U8 and the north-south S-Bahn tunnel. East Germany could not close the lines without splitting West Berlin's own network, so the trains kept running while the stations in the East Berlin section were bricked up, fenced off and guarded. West Berlin trains slowed through them without opening a door. Berliners called them Geisterbahnhöfe, ghost stations.

items.q: Which Berlin stations were ghost stations?
items.a: On the U6: Schwartzkopffstraße, Naturkundemuseum, Oranienburger Tor, Französische Straße and Stadtmitte. On the U8: Bernauer Straße, Rosenthaler Platz, Weinmeisterstraße, Alexanderplatz and Jannowitzbrücke. In the north-south S-Bahn tunnel: Nordbahnhof, Oranienburger Straße, Brandenburger Tor and Potsdamer Platz. Bornholmer Straße makes fifteen, and it was the only one that was not underground.

items.q: Can you still visit the Berlin ghost stations today?
items.a: You use them without noticing. Fourteen of the fifteen are ordinary working stations now, renovated and relit, so there is nothing eerie left on the platforms themselves. The exception worth planning a visit around is Nordbahnhof, where a free permanent exhibition on the mezzanine level explains the whole system. It needs no ticket and can be seen during normal S-Bahn operating hours.

Friedrichstraße sat inside East Berlin but stayed open because West Berlin passengers needed it to change between the U6 and the S-Bahn. They did that inside a sealed transfer area they could not leave, roughly the way you can change planes at an airport without entering the country. It was also a real border crossing. The separate departure hall beside it, the Tränenpalast or Palace of Tears, is still standing, is free to enter, and is closed on Mondays.

items.q: When did the Berlin ghost stations reopen?
items.a: One at a time, over more than two years. Jannowitzbrücke on the U8 was first on 11 November 1989, two days after the border opened. Rosenthaler Platz followed on 22 December 1989 and Bernauer Straße on 12 April 1990. The remaining U6 and U8 stations reopened together on 1 July 1990. Potsdamer Platz was last, on 3 March 1992, because the whole north-south tunnel had to be rebuilt first.

items.q: Is any Berlin ghost station still closed?
items.a: Yes, but not for Cold War reasons. Französische Straße on the U6 closed permanently on 3 December 2020. The new Unter den Linden station opened the following day one stop up the line as the interchange between the U5 and U6, and Französische Straße was no longer needed. If you go looking for it, get off at Unter den Linden instead.

Bornholmer Straße was the only ghost station above ground, and the Bösebrücke bridge over it carried the border crossing between Prenzlauer Berg and Wedding. On the evening of 9 November 1989 a crowd built up demanding to be let through, and at around 21:20 the guards here became the first to open a checkpoint and let people walk into West Berlin. The Wall did not fall everywhere at once. It gave way here first.

items.q: What is the best way to see this history in one morning?
items.a: Take the S-Bahn to Nordbahnhof, spend about twenty minutes at the free ghost stations exhibition on the mezzanine, then walk straight up onto Bernauer Straße for the Berlin Wall Memorial. You go from how the tunnels were sealed to how the surface was sealed in about ninety seconds of walking, and the whole thing costs nothing beyond your transport ticket.
